summary: Apple announced it's moving WWDC back to the McEnery Convention Center in San Jose after years in San Francisco, with dates set for June 5-9, 2017. Gruber, who spoke with Phil Schiller about the move, analyzes the practical advantages: proximity to Apple's headquarters means more engineers can attend, hotel costs are lower for attendees, and WWDC will be the dominant event in San Jose rather than a small fish in San Francisco's convention pond. He notes the move reflects Apple's changed position — they no longer need to go where the attention is, because the attention comes to them. While acknowledging nostalgia for the San Francisco era, Gruber sees clear wins for Apple itself but is uncertain whether the social scene around the conference will thrive in San Jose.
key_points: San Jose's proximity to Apple HQ means more employees from more teams can participate without losing entire days to travel, particularly benefiting developer labs
WWDC will dominate San Jose in a way it never could in San Francisco, where conferences like Oracle OpenWorld dwarf it at 60,000+ attendees
The move reflects Apple's transformed status: in 2003 they went to San Francisco seeking media attention, but today the attention comes to Apple wherever they are
San Jose is significantly cheaper for attendees, though hotels closest to the convention center still run around $350/night
The social scene is the biggest unknown — San Jose has a reputation as a sleepy work-oriented city that gets quiet after 5 pm
money_quotes: I don't know why Apple moved WWDC to San Francisco in 2003. But my guess is that they sought more media attention. Apple went to where the attention was. Today, the attention comes to Apple.
They could hold WWDC in the middle of a desert and it would still sell out in an instant and there'd be the same convoy of media trucks outside the hall the morning of the keynote.
If a large corporation can be described as a homebody, Apple is it. And San Francisco is not Apple's home turf.
In San Francisco, WWDC attendees are a small school of like-minded fish in a big pond. In San Jose, they'll own the pond.
WWDC is the biggest event of the year for the Apple world. But for the city of San Francisco, it's just another conference at Moscone.
An investment in Apple stock in 2002 would have increased around a hundredfold if held through today.
"It feels like WWDC is going home," he told me.
key_insight: Apple's decision to move WWDC back to San Jose is a quiet testament to how completely the company's fortunes have reversed — they no longer need to chase attention in a big city because the world now comes to them.
